characters.description: Avar warrior and former naib of Shamil who has defected to the Russians. Proud, watchful, and strategic; seeks Russian help to destroy Shamil and free his family.
Young Russian prince and officer at Vozdvizhensk who personally receives Hadji Murad's surrender. Son of the Viceroy; anxious about protocol but ambitious.
Elegant wife of young Vorontsov. Charms Hadji Murad, defuses the conflict with Meller-Zakomelsky, and mediates the social scene.
Six-year-old curly-headed stepson of young Vorontsov. Admires Hadji Murad's dagger and receives it as a kunak gift.
Russian general commanding at Vozdvizhensk, offended that Hadji Murad's surrender bypassed him. Pompous and status-obsessed, ultimately placated by Marya Vasilevna.
Model-looking little lady, wife of the general, briefly present during the confrontation.
One of Hadji Murad's murids. Reports on the state of the horses and men after the surrender.
Russian private from the Kurin regiment, mortally wounded in a skirmish. Dies in the fort hospital; had enlisted in place of his married brother.
Comrade of Avdeev who sits with him at his deathbed and records his last message home.
Fellow soldier who volunteers to write Avdeev's final letter home.
Russian officer who visits the dying Avdeev in the hospital to inquire after his soldier.
Peasant father of Peter Avdeev, head of the Kurenkov household. Regrets Peter's conscription and nags his elder son Akim.
Peter's elder brother, a family man of four children, in whose stead Peter enlisted. Drinks and is compared unfavorably to his brother.
Peter's mother, who dictates a tearful letter with a ruble enclosed to her son. Grieves publicly and arranges a requiem after news of his death.
Akim's wife, threshing at dawn with the family. Tries to smooth over father-son conflict.
Peter Avdeev's young wife. Has left the Kurenkov household to live with a shopman by whom she is pregnant; secretly relieved by Peter's death.
Aksinya's little orphaned child mentioned in her lament for Peter.
Aging Russian Viceroy of the Caucasus, father of young Vorontsov. English-educated, ambitious courtier who receives Hadji Murad in Tiflis and distrusts him privately while flattering him publicly.
Wife of the Viceroy Vorontsov. Hosts the Tiflis dinner and evening ball and greets Hadji Murad through the interpreter.
Handsome Georgian princess of about forty-five who dines beside Vorontsov and tactfully deflects the carroty general's dangerous anecdote about Dargo.
French friend of Princess Vorontsova at the Tiflis dinner; reacts in French to the Akhmet Khan widow story.
Aide-de-camp and physician at Vorontsov's court noted for his great behind-the-scenes influence. Partner at the ombre table.

aliases: the carroty general with the bristly mustaches
characters.description: Visiting Russian general whose tactless Dargo 'rescue' anecdote embarrasses Vorontsov at dinner. Returns next day in full uniform to take leave.
Refined flatterer at Vorontsov's table who redirects the conversation to Hadji Murad's exploits and compares him to Napoleon.
Plays ombre with Vorontsov after dinner; learned the game from the prince's valet.
Imam of the mountaineers and Hadji Murad's sworn enemy. Holds Hadji Murad's family hostage; referenced but not present in these chapters.

aliases: general in command of the left flank
characters.description: Russian general at Grozny commanding the left flank. Referenced as the recipient of Vorontsov's reports.
Deceased Avar rival who calumniated Hadji Murad to General Klugenau, forcing him to flee to Shamil. His widow was carried off by Hadji Murad.
Russian general who formerly arrested Hadji Murad on Akhmet Khan's denunciation, precipitating his flight to Shamil.
Russian Tsar referenced as the ultimate authority to whom Hadji Murad pledges service and to whom Vorontsov will appeal for pardon.
Former Tsar whose portrait decorates Vorontsov's gold snuff-box; purely iconographic appearance.
Russian colonel killed by Hadji Murad during General Pahlen's 1843 campaign, mentioned in the carroty general's anecdote.
Russian commander whose 1843 detachment was attacked by Hadji Murad after the capture of Gergebel.
Vorontsov's official interpreter in Tiflis who mediates between Hadji Murad and the Viceroy.
Vorontsov's aide-de-camp placed in charge of Hadji Murad. Accompanies him to the opera and the ball.
Local Kumyk noble in the Viceroy's waiting room lodging a complaint against a police official.

fusion_hints.reason: Both are Avdeev's comrades at the deathbed with nearly interchangeable functions (sit with him, take the letter). Merging into a single comrade tightens the hospital scene without losing emotional weight.

Both are minor courtier figures at Vorontsov's Tiflis table serving the same narrative function (flatterers/atmosphere). A single composite Caucasian courtier can carry the Napoleon-flattery beat and the ombre game.

Both are elite women at the Vorontsov dinner who react to the Hadji Murad stories. Orbelyani already carries the functional beat (deflecting the Dargo anecdote); Choiseuil's 'Quelle horreur!' can be absorbed.

Both serve as Vorontsov's buffer to Hadji Murad in Tiflis (interpreter vs. aide-de-camp). Consolidating into one handler keeps Hadji Murad's Russian-court arc legible. Caveat: Loris-Melikov is historically significant and may warrant preservation for source accuracy.

Both are mid-level Russian generals in the chain of custody over Hadji Murad after surrender. Kozlovsky is only a name on a report; his function can collapse into Meller-Zakomelsky or be cut entirely.
Both exist only in backstory exposition about why Hadji Murad fled to Shamil. A single antagonist figure (or a single line of dialogue) can compress this backstory beat.

Both are female voices in the Avdeev family chapter whose lines smooth over the father-son conflict or lament Peter. Consolidating into one matriarch figure keeps the peasant-family beat without three speaking women.
Both appear only inside the carroty general's single anecdote as names attached to Hadji Murad's past exploit. One name suffices; the other can be cut.
